front: What is the formal definition of an inner product space?
back: An inner product space is a vector space V over a field F (usually ℝ or ℂ) equipped with an inner product ⟨·, ·⟩: V × V → F. This operation must satisfy four axioms for all vectors u, v, w in V and scalar c in F: conjugate symmetry (⟨u, v⟩ = conjugate(⟨v, u⟩)), linearity in the first argument (⟨cu + v, w⟩ = c⟨u, w⟩ + ⟨v, w⟩), positive-definiteness (⟨v, v⟩ ≥ 0 and ⟨v, v⟩ = 0 iff v = 0).

front: Define the norm (or length) of a vector in an inner product space.
back: The norm (or length) of a vector v in an inner product space is defined as ||v|| = √⟨v, v⟩. This is a generalization of the Euclidean length of a vector in ℝⁿ.

front: What is the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality and what is its significance?
back: The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality states that for any two vectors u and v in an inner product space, |⟨u, v⟩| ≤ ||u|| ||v||. Its significance is that it guarantees the cosine of the angle between two vectors (defined as cosθ = ⟨u, v⟩/(||u|| ||v||)) is between -1 and 1, and it is fundamental in proving the triangle inequality.

front: What does it mean for two vectors to be orthogonal in an inner product space?
back: Two vectors u and v are orthogonal if their inner product is zero: ⟨u, v⟩ = 0. This generalizes the concept of perpendicular vectors from Euclidean geometry to abstract vector spaces.

front: What is an orthonormal set (or basis) and why is it important?
back: An orthonormal set is a set of vectors that are all mutually orthogonal and each has a norm of 1 (i.e., ⟨u_i, u_j⟩ = δ_ij, the Kronecker delta). An orthonormal basis is important because it simplifies calculations: representing a vector in this basis is done by taking inner products (v = Σ ⟨v, u_i⟩ u_i), and finding coordinates does not require solving a system of equations.
